text.headline: A Brief History of the Web
text.text: Key moments in the evolution of the World Wide Web, from its invention at CERN to the modern era of browsers and open standards.

text.headline: The Web is Proposed
text.text: Tim Berners-Lee submits "Information Management: A Proposal" to his supervisor at CERN. The memo describes a global hypertext system linking documents across networked computers.

text.headline: First Website Goes Live
text.text: Berners-Lee publishes the first website at CERN, describing the Web project itself. The site runs on a NeXT computer and is still accessible today at its original URL.

text.headline: NCSA Mosaic
text.text: The NCSA Mosaic browser, developed at the University of Illinois, becomes the first browser to display images inline with text. It sparks explosive public interest in the Web.

text.headline: W3C Founded
text.text: Berners-Lee founds the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) at MIT to develop open standards for the Web. The W3C's mission: "to lead the Web to its full potential."

text.headline: JavaScript Invented
text.text: Brendan Eich creates JavaScript at Netscape in ten days. Originally called Mocha, then LiveScript, it becomes the programming language of the Web.

text.headline: Firefox 1.0
text.text: Mozilla releases Firefox, reviving open-source competition in the browser market after years of Internet Explorer dominance. Firefox 1.0 ships in November 2004 and gains 100 million downloads in a year.

text.headline: Chrome Launches
text.text: Google releases Chrome, built on the V8 JavaScript engine and the WebKit rendering engine. Its multi-process architecture and speed benchmarks shift the competitive landscape.

text.headline: HTML5 Standardized
text.text: The W3C publishes HTML5 as an official recommendation, formalizing native video, canvas drawing, offline storage, and semantic elements — ending the era of Flash plugins.
